settings.title: Settings
settings.privacy_text: Your data is stored exclusively on your computer in SQLite format. No information is sent to external servers.
settings.about_desc: Body composition analysis via BLE bioimpedance.
settings.about_copyright: © 2026 — No login, no ads, no servers.
settings.delete_all_confirm: Are you sure you want to delete ALL your data?
settings.delete_all_warning: This includes all profiles, measurements and settings. This action CANNOT be undone.
metric.pending_desc: Complete your profile (Age, Height, Sex) in the 'Profile' tab so we can calculate this metric.
desc.bmi: BMI is calculated by dividing your weight (kg) by your height squared (m²). It's the main worldwide indicator for weight classification. Below 18.5 = underweight, 18.5–24.9 = ideal, 25–29.9 = overweight, above 30 = obese.
desc.body_fat: The percentage of your total weight made up of fat. Measured via bioelectrical impedance (painless current through your feet). Ideal values vary by sex and age. Too low affects hormones; too high increases risk of heart disease and diabetes.
desc.fat_mass: Total weight of fat in your body in kilograms. Calculated from body fat percentage times total weight. Useful for tracking actual fat loss (not just weight loss).
desc.subcutaneous_fat: Fat located just beneath the skin that you can pinch. Represents about 80% of total body fat. Less dangerous than visceral fat, but excess contributes to joint problems and being overweight.
desc.visceral_fat: Fat accumulated around internal organs (liver, intestines, kidneys). It's the most dangerous fat for health. Level up to 9 = healthy. 10–14 = high, increase physical activity. Above 14 = elevated risk of cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes.
desc.muscle_mass: Percentage of your body composed of muscle tissue (skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscles). More muscle means higher calorie burn at rest and better mobility. Low values indicate a sedentary lifestyle or age-related muscle loss (sarcopenia).
desc.muscle_mass_kg: Total weight of muscle tissue in kilograms. Tracking this helps verify if you're gaining muscle (not just weight). Combined with body fat, it gives a complete picture of body composition.
desc.smm_percent: Skeletal muscle mass percentage — the muscles attached to bones that you use to move (arms, legs, back). Calculated using the Janssen formula (2000), validated against MRI. The most accurate indicator of functional strength.
desc.smm: Weight of skeletal muscle mass in kg. These are the voluntary muscles that control movement. Higher values indicate better physical capability and lower risk of falls and fractures in older age.
desc.lbm: Lean body mass = total weight minus all fat. Includes muscles, bones, organs, and water. It's your body weight if you had 0% fat. The higher it is, the better your basal metabolism.
desc.body_water: Percentage of your body composed of water (inside and outside cells). Water is essential for all bodily functions. Low values indicate dehydration. Ideal range is 50–65% for men and 45–60% for women.
desc.water_mass: Total volume of water in your body in kilograms. Includes water in blood, muscles, organs, and between cells. Varies throughout the day based on hydration, diet, and exercise.
desc.bone_mass: Estimated weight of your skeleton (bone minerals). Measured by bioimpedance with ±0.3 kg accuracy compared to DEXA (X-ray). Important to monitor after age 40, as bone loss increases the risk of osteoporosis and fractures.
desc.protein: Protein percentage in your body. Proteins form the building blocks of muscles, enzymes, and antibodies. Low values may indicate poor protein intake or muscle loss. Ideal is above 16%, with 20–24% being excellent.
desc.bmr: Basal Metabolic Rate — how many calories your body burns per day at absolute rest (sleeping). Calculated using the Mifflin-St Jeor formula with weight, height, age, and sex. The more muscle you have, the higher your BMR and the more calories you naturally burn.
desc.obesity_percent: How much your current weight is above or below ideal weight (calculated with BMI 22). Negative = below ideal, 0–10% = healthy range, above 10% = overweight. Helps understand how much adjustment is needed to reach a healthy weight.
desc.body_score: Overall health score from 0 to 100, calculated by combining 5 metrics: body fat, muscle mass, visceral fat, body water, and BMI. Below 40 = needs improvement, 40–60 = fair, 60–80 = good, above 80 = excellent.
desc.ideal_weight: Your ideal weight calculated at the center of the healthy BMI range (22 kg/m²). Current difference: {diff} kg. Negative values mean you're below ideal. This is a general guide — athletes and muscular people may be healthy above this weight.
desc.metabolic_age: Your metabolic age compares your metabolism with the population average. If your metabolic age is lower than your real age, your metabolism is more efficient than average. Values above your real age indicate a slower metabolism, usually due to excess fat and low muscle.
desc.ffmi: Fat-Free Mass Index (FFM divided by height²). Measures how much muscle you have relative to your height. Up to 20 = normal, 20–25 = athletic. Above 25 in men is exceptional (elite athlete level). Helps evaluate muscle gain independently of total weight.
desc.smi: Skeletal Muscle Index — limb muscle mass divided by height². Used to diagnose sarcopenia (pathological muscle loss) per European EWGSOP2 criteria. Low values in older adults indicate risk of falls, fractures, and loss of independence.
desc.whr: Ratio between waist and hip measurements. WHO indicator for fat distribution. Fat concentrated in the belly (apple shape) is more dangerous than in the hips (pear shape). Men above 0.90 and women above 0.85 have increased cardiovascular risk.
desc.whtr: Ratio between waist and height. Simple rule: your waist should measure less than half your height. Below 0.5 = healthy. Above 0.5 = increased risk of heart disease. Considered by many researchers better than BMI for predicting health risks.
report.weight_tip_lose: To reach your ideal weight, you need to lose {diff}Kg. It is recommended to control your diet and moderately increase exercise.
report.weight_tip_gain: To reach your ideal weight, you need to gain {diff}Kg. It is recommended to increase caloric intake with nutritious foods.
report.weight_tip_ideal: Congratulations! You are at your ideal weight. Keep maintaining your healthy habits.
body_type_desc.athlete_body: Your body type is athletic, with high muscle mass and low body fat. You are in excellent shape! Continue with your training and balanced nutrition to maintain this level.
body_type_desc.muscular_obesity: You have a lot of muscle mass, but also excess fat. Focus on reducing body fat while maintaining strength exercises. Adjust your diet to create a moderate caloric deficit.
body_type_desc.obesity: Your body type is overweight, with elevated weight and body fat. It is recommended to increase the amount of exercise, combining aerobic and strength exercises, and try to eat less fatty foods. Combine exercise with protein intake to increase muscle mass and improve your basal metabolic rate. Obesity carries a higher risk of chronic diseases. Take action before it becomes uncontrollable!
body_type_desc.muscular: You have a well-developed muscular body. Your body composition is good with above-average muscle mass. Maintain your activity level and protein-rich diet.
body_type_desc.healthy: Congratulations! You have a healthy body type with a good proportion between muscle and fat. Keep maintaining your exercise habits and balanced nutrition.
body_type_desc.slightly_overweight: You are slightly above your ideal weight. Small diet adjustments and increased regular physical activity can make a big difference. Focus on combined exercises (aerobic + strength).
body_type_desc.lean_muscular: You have a lean and muscular body. Good definition with low fat. To maintain, continue with strength training and adequate protein intake.
body_type_desc.lean: You have a lean body with average fat and muscle. To improve composition, consider adding strength exercises to gain muscle mass.
body_type_desc.hidden_obesity: Despite a normal BMI, your body fat percentage is high. This is called 'hidden obesity'. It is recommended to start strength exercises to increase muscle mass and reduce body fat.
body_type_desc.skeletal_lean: You are very underweight with little muscle mass. It is important to increase caloric intake with nutritious foods and start a strength exercise program to gain muscle mass.
body_type_desc.slightly_underweight: You are slightly underweight. Moderately increase caloric intake and consider strength exercises to gain muscle mass in a healthy way.
